title: When Using AI Leads to “Brain Fry”
subtitle: A new study finds that certain patterns of AI use are driving cognitive fatigue, while others can help reduce burnout.

content_blocks.title: The Impact of Intensive AI Oversight

items.label: Increase in mental effort for workers with high degrees of AI oversight
Greater information overload predicted by intensive AI monitoring
Increase in decision fatigue for workers experiencing AI brain fry
Higher frequency of major errors among those with AI-associated mental strain
Reduction in burnout scores when AI replaces routine or repetitive tasks

content_blocks.title: Distinguishing Burnout from AI Brain Fry

items.a: State of chronic workplace stress consisting of emotional exhaustion.
items.b: Acute mental fatigue from oversight of AI tools beyond cognitive capacity.

items.a: Alleviated when AI replaces tiring, repetitive, or unenjoyable tasks.
items.b: Worsened by high degrees of AI monitoring and increased sphere of accountability.

items.a: Reduced by 15% through the elimination of 'toil'.
items.b: Associated with a 'buzzing' feeling, mental fog, and headaches.

content_blocks.title: Key Findings on AI Integration and Productivity
points: Productivity gains typically peak at two to three simultaneous AI tools; beyond three tools, productivity scores begin to dip due to multitasking costs.
Managerial support is a critical buffer: workers whose managers answer AI questions report 15% lower mental fatigue.
Team-level 'AI orphan taxes' occur when individuals are expected to figure out AI alone, leading to higher measurable fatigue scores.
The prevalence of 'AI brain fry' varies significantly by role, with marketing (26%) and engineering among the highest, and legal (6%) among the lowest.
